coredata.dc:title: Cynodon dactylon derived fluorescent N-doped carbon dots: Implications of photocatalytic and biological applications
coredata.dc:creator: Qurtulen,
Ahmad, Anees
Shahraki, Hesam Salimi
Shakeel, Nimra
Bushra, Rani
coredata.dc:description: CQDs possess various appealing properties like excellent light harvesting capability, unique photo-induced electron transfer ability, low toxicity, and good biocompatibility. This work aims to demonstrate the potential of CQDs from natural waste resources in wastewater treatments and biomedical applications. Herein we report the eco-friendly approach for preparing nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) using the Cynodon dactylon (grass) as a carbon precursor via a highly effective hydrothermal process. The structural aspects and chemical nature of N-CQDs were considered using FTIR, XRD, SEM & EDX, TEM, AFM, EPR, and XPS. In addition, UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy elucidated the optical performance of N-CQDs. N-CQDs possess stable fluorescent properties, good biocompatibility, and acceptable quantum yield (24.44%). In this study, five dyes, namely Toluidine Blue (TB), Amido Black (AB), Congo Red (CR), and Xylol Orange (XO), were chosen to investigate the photocatalytic performance of the nanomaterial under UV–vis light irradiation. The synthesized N-CQDs exhibited remarkable photocatalytic activity for various model dyes of varying concentrations (5, 10, 50, and 100 ppm), highlighting the pivotal role of N-CQDs in promoting the separation migration efficiency of photoexcited e −- h + pairs. Moreover, the impact of pH, contact time, and amount of catalyst on the degradation of dyes were also examined. Additionally, we investigated the interaction of N-CQDs with lysozyme protein using fluorescence quenching. Thus, the developed nanomaterial efficiently treats wastewater and holds great promise in biosensing applications.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: CQDs possess various appealing properties like excellent light harvesting capability, unique photo-induced electron transfer ability, low toxicity, and good biocompatibility. This work aims to demonstrate the potential of CQDs from natural waste resources in wastewater treatments and biomedical applications. Herein we report the eco-friendly approach for preparing nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) using the Cynodon dactylon (grass) as a carbon precursor via a highly effective hydrothermal process. The structural aspects and chemical nature of N-CQDs were considered using FTIR, XRD, SEM & EDX, TEM, AFM, EPR, and XPS. In addition, UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy elucidated the optical performance of N-CQDs. N-CQDs possess stable fluorescent properties, good biocompatibility, and acceptable quantum yield (24.44%). In this study, five dyes, namely Toluidine Blue (TB), Amido Black (AB), Congo Red (CR), and Xylol Orange (XO), were chosen to investigate the photocatalytic performance of the nanomaterial under UV–vis light irradiation. The synthesized N-CQDs exhibited remarkable photocatalytic activity for various model dyes of varying concentrations (5, 10, 50, and 100 ppm), highlighting the pivotal role of N-CQDs in promoting the separation migration efficiency of photoexcited e −- h + pairs. Moreover, the impact of pH, contact time, and amount of catalyst on the degradation of dyes were also examined. Additionally, we investigated the interaction of N-CQDs with lysozyme protein using fluorescence quenching. Thus, the developed nanomaterial efficiently treats wastewater and holds great promise in biosensing applications.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: A new class of fluorescent nanomaterials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), possess numerous applications because of their characteristic properties like high stability, conductivity, less toxicity, biocompatibility, low cost, chemical inertness, and easy functionalization [1–3]. It has comparable optical properties to traditional quantum dots [4]. Due to their tunable and robust fluorescence emission properties, their application in biomedicine [5], optoelectronics [6–8], optical bioimaging [9], catalysis [10], sensing [11], solar cells [12], and as a filler in electromagnetic composite [13–16] was examined. CQDs are spherical, zero-dimensional carbon nanoparticles, significantly less in diameter ranging from 1 to 10 nm in size, and water-soluble.

Semiconductor quantum dots are widely used for metal ion detection because they have significant potential in optical sensing and a size-tunable absorption and emission profile [17]. Despite their toxicity, even at low concentrations, their applications are limited [18,19]. This prompted the creation of CQDs that substitute semiconductor quantum dots. Since CQDs have many significant properties like stable multicolor emission, abundant polar functional groups, water-solubility, simple preparation, and ease of functionalization. Biomass-based nanomaterials are becoming increasingly important to scientists and environmentalists due to their propensity for being more affordable and environmentally sustainable. It is widely distributed, easily accessible, and a significant renewable carbon source. Researchers have been working on producing carbon nanomaterials derived from various biomass [20–22]. Unlike other carbon nanomaterials, cheap and renewable resources can be used to produce CQDs [23]. Recent reported studies focused on synthesizing CQDs with sp2 conjugated π domain using natural resources including orange waste [24], green tea leaf [25], banana peel [26], waste paper [27], plastic cups [28], grass [29], neem leaves [30], fennel seed [31], milk [32] sugarcane waste [33], tamarind [34], and cabbage [35], etc. Therefore, it is vital to synthesize high-quality CQDs from a low-cost source through an environmentally friendly route. So far, many methods have been pursued in their synthesis, like hydrothermal treatment, laser ablation, electrochemical techniques, etc. Among all scenarios, the most effective approach for synthesizing CQDs from biomass-based material is the hydrothermal method, wherein mild experimental conditions are required with a facile instrumentation technique and high atom economy [36]. Besides, this method provides efficient dehydration, polymerization, and carbonization effects toward the natural precursors.

At present, water pollution is a significant issue that needs worldwide attention. The release of industrial effluents into the water bodies further worsens the rising problem of the water crisis [37,38]. In the scientific field, efforts have been made to remove the hazardous pollutants from wastewater [39–41]. Dyes released from various industries are carcinogenic, mutagenic, and genotoxic, severely affecting living organisms and ecosystems [42]. In addition, dyes are biodegradation resistant. Many conventional techniques are utilized to remove the wastewater's organic pollutants, but these generate toxic intermediates [43,44]. Photocatalytic technology received considerable attention during the last decade because it is a powerful and environmentally friendly approach for destructing organic contaminants by high oxidative reactive oxygen species (ROS) without producing secondary waste [45–48]. Moreover, this technology offers a promising strategy for addressing several environmental issues. The dire need for time is to explore a photocatalyst that is stable, renewable, and sustainable and has the potential to degrade organic dyes with high photocatalytic performance. Over the past few years, CQDs have been used in bio-imaging, fluorescent sensing, photocatalysis, and electrocatalytic applications, owing to their significant characteristic properties. Still, few reports on their application in the photocatalytic degradation of pollutants are available.

Despite many fascinating properties, unmodified carbon dots showed poor photosensitization and spectra efficiency [49]. Hence, to circumvent this issue, the nanomaterial structure has been modified by doping with a heteroatom, which helps enhance the PL properties of carbon dots [50]. Incorporating hetero atoms (nitrogen, sulfur, phosphorous, boron, etc.) into the CDs modulates their electronic transport and optical properties. Doping is an effective strategy to enhance quantum efficiency. Nitrogen is considered an ideal dopant because of its high resemblance with carbon in atomic size and electronic structure. It is incorporated during synthesis using the precursor containing nitrogen or post-synthetic functionalization methodologies [51]. Inspired by this fact, plentiful nitrogen-rich natural resources have been utilized to prepare CQDs [52]. CQDs synthesized from natural sources have gained immense popularity due to their high carbon and nitrogen content, inexpensive, excellent photoactivity, and non-toxicity.

Due to their unique characteristic properties, CDs are a promising candidate for various biomedical applications, including biosensors, drug delivery, biological pH probe, protein, and nucleic acid analysis [53–55]. For biological applications, the fluorescence properties of CDs are of utmost importance. The immobilization of biomolecules on CDs causes alteration in their fluorescent property. Therefore, the conjugation of CDs with biomolecules is beneficial to enhance their properties for many biomedical applications. Recently, protein-conjugated CDs have gained importance in the biomedical field [56–58]. The protein is a supporting agent and carry drugs, antibodies, and other molecules to the biological system. Many analytical techniques have been used to study the interaction between CDs and proteins [59–61]. Among them, fluorescence correlation spectroscopy is most widely used to investigate the interactions because of its high sensitivity to the microenvironment [62].

Herein, we propose the utilization of grass (high content of C and N) as the starting material for the production N-CQDs using the hydrothermal method. This synthesis follows the principles of green synthesis as it is cost-effective, has no toxic chemicals, is eco-friendly, has a short-time reaction, and uses water as a solvent. The presence of N in N-CQDs improved the charge delocalization and reduced work function and energy transfer process in the nanomaterial, making them suitable for photocatalytic applications [63]. This study aims to convert the biomass into valuable CQDs and use them as photocatalysts for environmental remediation to degrade the various hazardous dyes under UV–Vis light irradiation. Different characterization techniques are employed for investigating the morphology, chemical composition, and size distribution of N-CQDs. A plausible photocatalytic mechanism of N-CQDs for the degradation of dyes is proposed. Moreover, we also investigated the binding interaction between N-CQDs and lysozyme protein inspired by the growing interest in CDs with high biocompatibility. The understanding of this interaction is beneficial in the biomedical and bioengineering field.

Only two materials were used for the preparation of N-CQDs. Grass as the green precursor collected from the Aligarh Muslim University Campus, Aligarh, and Water. Toluidine Blue, Amido Black, Congo Red, and Xylol Orange, were bought from Sigma Aldrich, India. Distilled water was used for making the aqueous solution of the dyes. Functional groups of N-CQDs were analyzed using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (Model Spectrum-BX, USA), and Raman microscopy (Renishaw inVia 2000 system, UK), while the structure of the compound is determined by X-ray diffraction (PW-1148/89 Netherlands with CuKa radiation). N-CQDs' morphology and elemental composition were confirmed with JSM, 6510LV, JEOL, Japan, scanning electron microscope. Transmission electron microscopic images were captured on TEM2100, JEOL, Japan operated at 200 kV. NT-MDT-INTEGRA and Zetasizer Nano ZS analyzed the surface topography and surface charge of N-CQDs. UV–Vis. Spectrometer (PerkinElmer) is used for recording the UV–Vis spectra. The chemical structure and composition of N-CQDs were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy on Theroscientific (Portland, OR, USA) with K-Alpha monochromatic source. The electron paramagnetic resonance technique used EMXmicro A200–9.5/12/S/W (BRUKER BIOSPIN, Germany) to determine the unpaired electron in N-CQDs.

200 mg of grass was thoroughly washed to remove the impurities. After washing, the grass was dried in the sunlight, ground, and powdered in the mortar. The obtained powder was mixed with distilled water (100 mL) and kept in stainless steel autoclave. Later on, the mixture was heated for 90 min at 200 °C. Finally, the synthesized powder was filtered, washed, and dried in the oven at 60°C for 24 h to produce N-CQDs (Fig. S1) [29].

The quantum yield (QY) was determined using the reported method [64]. Typically, N-CQDs are diluted with ultrapure water with a refractive index of 1.33. For quantum yield measurements, quinine was used as a standard reference. Quinine solution was diluted by using H2SO4 solution (QY = 54%, η = 1.33). At their respective excitation wavelength, the absorbance and fluorescence of CDs were recorded. The absorption value of both CDs and quinine solution was maintained at 0.05 in a 1 cm quartz cuvette to minimize the interference of self-absorption. Finally, using the following Eq. (1), QY was calculated:

where, Q refers to the QY value, I, A, and η refer to the measured integrated emission intensity, absorbance, and refractive index of the solvent, respectively. The subscripts R and CD represent the standard and carbon dots.

N-CQDs photocatalytic performance was examined by monitoring the photodegradation of dyes (TB, XO, AB, and CR) in an aqueous solution at different experimental conditions under UV light illumination. The sample (5 mL) was withdrawn at multiple time intervals, and the degradation efficiency of synthesized materials was evaluated by measuring the absorbance of dye using a UV-visible spectrophotometer at their characteristic absorption maxima [65]. All the experiments were carried out in a triplicate manner.

The binding experiments were conducted on a fluorescence spectrometer (Model RF-5301PC, Shimadzu, Japan) with a xenon lamp (150 W). Slit widths (5 nm) were used for excitation and emission, and the temperature was set to 298 K. The lysozyme protein (HSL) solution was prepared in a phosphate buffer (pH 7.4). Before the experiment, the solution of protein and N-CQDs-HSL complex was equilibrated. The binding of N-CQDs with lysozyme protein was analyzed by observing the change in the initial fluorescence of the HSL on adding N-CQDs of various concentrations (1–10 µM) at an excitation wavelength of 280 nm. The spectra were recorded between 300 and 400 nm [56].

FTIR spectra confirmed the presence of specific functional groups on the surface of the prepared N-CQDs (Fig. 1(a) ). The peaks observed at 3416 and 2919 cm−1 in grass powder authenticate the presence of O-H and C-H bonding, bearing witness to carbohydrates and lignin [66]. The absorption band in grass powder at 1643, 1391, and 1024 cm−1 can be attributed to C=O (carboxyl/carbonyl), C=C, and C-H bending [67].

The broad peak observed at 3348 cm−1 evidenced -OH and N-H stretching vibrations, implying the presence of hydroxyl and amino groups on the surface of N-CQDs [67]. It is noted that the characteristic bending vibration band of C-H and -CH2- was observed at 2919 and 1452 cm−1, which collectively showed the presence of alkyl and alkene groups. Meanwhile, 1658, 1055, and 787 cm−1 peaks correlated with the stretching vibration of C=O, C-O-C, and out-of-plane C-H bending vibration of the aromatic ring, respectively [68,69]. Moreover, the band at 1513 cm−1 related to the -NH2 (amide bending) is generally found in N-doped carbon dots [70]. It can be concluded that the functional group rich surface of N-CQDs represents the series of surface emissive sites accountable for the excellent PL properties of the carbon nanomaterial [71]. Besides, these functional groups are also responsible for the outstanding aqueous dispersibility of the carbon dots [72].

Fig. 1(b) depicts the XRD pattern of the synthesized N-CQDs. A broad diffraction peak centered at 22.4° could be assigned to graphitic carbon's (002) diffraction pattern that has been compatible with the JCPDS Card No. 75-0444 [73], indicating that carbon dots has a predominantly disordered amorphous graphitic structure [74]. The interlayer d-spacing value calculated using Bragg's equation was 0.71 nm higher than that of graphitic carbon (0.34 nm). The most significant lattice space compared to graphite may result from oxygen-carrying functional groups and the introduction of other defect sites through N-doping in the lattice [75]. The broad peak suggests that the synthesized doped CDs were amorphous.

The purity of the N-CQDs was confirmed by Raman spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 1c, two predominant peaks at 1368, and 1575 cm−1 are attributed to the D and G bands, respectively. The amount of graphitization and vibrations of disordered graphitic carbon represents the sp2 (G band) and sp3 (D band) hybridization associated with N-CQDs. The G and D bands are related to the E2g and A1g vibration modes. The intensity ratio (ID/IG), which was calculated from their maximum peak counts of D and G bands, was determined to be 0.91, indicating the purity of the sample [76]. The presence of G and D bands signifies the successful production of CDs with sp3 and sp2 hybridized matrix and core through a facile hydrothermal approach.

Zeta potential values were measured by the zeta sizer to determine the surface charge on N-CQDs. Fig. 1(d) shows a single peak of zeta potential (-6.25 mV) of peak width 10.6 mV, which affirms the presence of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the N-CQDs surface. The negative charge moieties on the surface impart excellent solubility of carbon dots in water and allow further functionalization with biomolecules [77].

For the determination of surface functional groups and elemental composition of the synthesized NCQDs, XPS analysis was performed. Fig 2a depicts three strong peaks, at 282.5 eV for carbon, 401 eV for nitrogen, and 530.25 eV for oxygen. The elemental composition of N-CQDs, as determined by XPS analysis, was found to be C (70%), O (20%), and N (10%), respectively. Four component peaks at 283.5, 285.3, 287 and 288 eV are observed in the high-resolution spectrum of C1s (Fig. 2b) which corresponds to the C-C/C = C, C-O, C-N, and C = O bond, respectively, suggested that the surface of N-CQDs have an abundance of hydrophilic groups [78–80]. The high-resolution spectra of O1s can be deconvoluted into two peaks centered at 531 eV and 533.2 eV, which were attributed to C = O and C-OH/C-O-C functional groups, respectively [81–83] (Fig 2c). Interestingly, the N1s spectrum reveals two peaks at 402 and 403.1 eV, reflecting the bonding structure of the N-H and C-N-C bond [84] (Fig. 2d). The analyzed XPS results are consistent with the FTIR study.

The EPR study was mostly carried out to determine the ground state properties of the carbon dots. The signal at g = 1.999 suggested the presence of singly occupied orbital in the ground state of CDs, which justified that CDs have both electron-donating and accepting properties [85] (Fig. S2).

SEM and TEM analysis examined N-CQDs morphology, as depicted in Fig. 3 . SEM micrographs of as-synthesized CDs have graphite-like continuous and folded layer structures. The graphite rods are peeled off into tiny particles or carbon dots. The folded layer is due to the presence of hydroxyl groups on their surface [86] (Fig. 3(a) and (b)). The high surface energy of the small-size carbon particles (determined from TEM studies < 10 nm) resulted in the agglomeration and forming a cluster-like structure. It can be concluded that the product has a vast surface area due to its small particle size, making them available for utilization in the removal of pollutants from wastewater. EDX spectrum of the synthesized N-CQDs reveals the presence of C, N, and O, with corresponding atomic percentages of 59.31, 5.85, and 34.83%, respectively (Fig. 3(c)).

Fig 3(d&e) showed the HRTEM images of N-CQDs, depicting that the obtained product was well dispersed. The amorphous clusters of CDs are nearly spherical with average size distribution estimated to be 3.4 nm using Image-J software, which showed that abundant surface area is available for adsorption of the dyes (Fig. 3g). Absence of fringes in high magnification image of one separate carbon dot (Fig. 3f) also confirms the amorphous nature of the nanomaterial. CDs fabricated from natural and synthetic carbon sources have an amorphous configuration [67,87].

AFM further traced the morphology and size of the N-CQDs to gain insight into their thickness. The morphological pattern of N-CQDs (Fig. 3(h and i)) suggests the thickness averaging around 7 nm, indicating the formation of multilayer CQDs [88]. It was observed that the synthesized N-CQDs are very small in size and spherical in shape, showing a small degree of agglomeration.

The absorption spectrum of N-CQDs showed two absorption peaks at 280 and 328 nm in the UV region, typically ascribing to the conjugated aromatic sp2 domains (π→π* transition) from the carbon core and -C= O functionality (n→π* transition) present on the surface of CQDs (Fig 4(a) ) [89]. As illustrated in Fig. 4(b), the band gap energy of the synthesized N-CQDs was determined by extrapolating the curve between (αhν)² versus hν. The computed value shows that the synthesized product has a bandgap of 2.6 eV, indicating that the product has a strong visible light response. The PL spectra of the synthesized N-CQDs showed an emission intensity peak at 420 nm at an excitation wavelength of 330 nm (Fig. 4(c)). Moreover, the PL spectra at various excitation wavelengths (300–400 nm) showed an increase in the peak intensity with the increasing wavelength (Fig. 4(d)). The emission spectra exhibit the red shift as the excitation wavelength increases from 300 to 400 nm, further demonstrating that the emission of N-CQDs is excitation-dependent [90]. Increased surface area, emissive traps, quantum effect, and radiative properties might be responsible for the high fluorescent nature of the N-CQDs [91].

The optical reflectance spectra of N-CQD were recorded using a UV–vis spectrometer. The light absorption properties of the synthesized N-CQDs were also explained by UV–vis DRS study in the wavelength ranging from 200 to 800 nm. The absorption peak of the synthesized product was shifted more towards the higher wavelength in the visible region, indicating that N-CQDs generate more electron-hole pairs upon excitation and result in higher photocatalytic activity [92] (Fig. S3(a)). The band gap energy was calculated by using Kubelka-Munk (KM) method, where KM function “((F(R))” is plotted versus photon energy “(hѵ)” (Fig. S3(b)). In the Tauc equation, α is replaced with (F(R)), and it is represented by Eqs. (2) and (3) [93]. The extrapolated intercept gives the Eg value of 1.43 eV for N-CQDs.

A UV-visible spectrophotometer evaluated the photocatalytic capability of the synthesized carbon nanomaterial by the degradation of TB, AB, CR, and XO dyes. The influence of the concentration of dye, amount of catalyst, contact time, and pH on the photodegradation of dyes was examined.

The excellent degradation behavior (Table S1) of the synthesized N-CQDs towards the various dyes is due to the participation of reactive oxide species in the photodegradation process. Initially, the effect of dye concentration on the degradation process at different time intervals by keeping other variables constant was examined. The different dye solutions of concentrations 5, 10, 50, and 100 ppm were prepared and analyzed for degradation properties. TB solution of 5, 10, and 50 ppm was observed to be completely decolorated in 5 min under UV irradiation in the presence of N-CQDs as a photocatalyst, with degradation efficiencies determined to be 98, 96.66, and 94.16%, respectively. While the complete degradation of 100 ppm required 30 min, with degradation efficiency noted to be 93% (Fig. 5 (a–d)). Similarly, 50 and 100 ppm solution of XO showed 74.12 and 73% degradation efficiencies in 30 and 35 min, while AB showed 75% in 20 and 30 min, respectively. In contrast, 5 and 10 ppm solutions of XO and AB are de-colorized in 5 and 10 min with degradation effeciency noted to be 90, 85.56, 90.13 and 88.11% respectively((Figs. 6 and 7
(a–d)). CR dye solution (5 ppm) in the presence of a catalyst showed a degradation efficiency of 90.76%. In contrast, their 10, 50, and 100 ppm solutions were degraded in 10, 30, and 60 min, with 90.13, 84.8, and 78.2% efficiencies, respectively ((Fig. 8 (a–d)).

It can be deduced from the results that dye concentration and photocatalytic degradation efficiency manifest an inverse relationship. In addition, at high dye concentrations, photons are intercepted by the dye molecules before reaching the catalyst's surface, resulting in lower production of hydroxy radicals and slowing down the degradation process [94]. The decolorization rate depends on hydroxyl radical formation, a critical species in the decolorization process. The results also demonstrated that as the concentration of the dye increases, contact time also increases. All dye solutions of concentrations 5 and 10 ppm are degraded in 5 and 10 min. The degradation of dyes (100 ppm) was observed at 30 min for TB and AB, 35 min for XO, and 60 min for CR. At higher concentrations, a longer time is needed to generate enough reactive species required to degrade the dye molecules. Moreover, the large surface area of the CQDs is also responsible for decomposing more dye molecules. Thus, it is concluded that the obtained N-CQDs have high photocatalytic activity under UV and visible light radiation.

The degradation behavior of different dyes at various concentrations is summarized in Fig 9 . Compared with the cationic dye (TB), the degradation efficiencies of anionic dyes(CR, AB & XO) were significantly less due to the negatively charged surface of CQDs that support the positively charged dye particles to interact, and resulted in an increase in the degradation efficiency [95–97]. In addition, the partial positive charge on the anionic dyes helps in partial binding with negatively charged CQDs, thus showing some degradation. Among the anionic dyes, CR and AB show the highest degradation because their structure consists of SO3− Na+ group that imparts a positive charge during its dissociation in water. The difference in degradation rates for different dyes is attributed to their chemical structures, resulting in different adsorption characteristics and susceptibility to photodegradation.

The selectivity and effectiveness of the material were demonstrated by examining the influence of the catalyst dose on the degradation process under ambient experimental conditions. With the increasing dosage (0.05–0.5 g) of N-CQDs (Fig. S4), the percent uptake of various dyes increases. The maximum degradation was observed at 0.1 g, and on further increasing the initial catalytic dosage, the degradation was slightly increased with no significance. Initially, when the amount of N-CQDs increases, the number of photons absorbed by the CDs increases, which leads to the enhanced production of ROS species and significantly contributes to dye degradation. Further increase in the amount of CDs has no significant effect on the degradation process because a large amount of the catalyst increases suspension turbidity, ultimately decreasing the penetration of light and making the degradation process less effective [98]. Therefore, 0.1 g of N-CQDs was considered the optimum dosage for the efficient degradation of dyes. pH played a crucial role in the dye degradation process. It impacts the electrostatic interactions between the dye and the catalyst, surface charge, generation of reactive species, and dye's chemical form [99]. The results illustrated that degradation efficiency decreases with increasing pH (4, 7, 9). At pH 4, the degradation capability of dyes (CR, AB, and XO) was decreased. A slight decrease was observed under neutral conditions (pH 7) (Fig 10 (b–d). A further reduction in the degradation of dyes was noticed under alkaline conditions (pH 9). At basic conditions, the surface of CDs acquired a negative charge due to the deprotonation of the functional moieties, resulting in the loss of electrostatic interaction between dye anions and the negatively charged surface of CDs. Moreover, excessive hydroxyl ions at high pH resulted in slowed down the degradation process. The negatively charged anions and the protonated functional groups of CDs at pH 4 facilitate the maximum degradation of dyes [100,101]. The degradation of TB dye was significantly enhanced at low and high pH values since TB has two pKa values (2.4 and 11.6) [102] ((Fig. 10(a)). Generally, excessive production of hydrogen and hydroxyl ions in the dye solution at low and high pH resulted in a reduction in the production of ROS species and hence a decrease in the decomposition rate of the dye molecules.

In addition, a comparison study has been made with other relevant works reported in the literature (Table 1 ). The synthesized N-CQDs showed the highest degradation efficiency against different dyes under UV-light illumination compared to other reported work. Thus, N-CQDs can be considered a cost-effective and efficient material to remove dyes from aqueous media.

To determine the photocorrosive stability, the synthesized NCQDs were examined by their recycling test. After its utilization in the degradation of dyes, N-CQDs were recovered by centrifugation and further utilized for recycling study. The findings showed that the degradation efficiency decreased significantly from 94 to 80% after four repeating cycles, indicating that NCQDs possess excellent stability during the photocatalytic process under UV light irradiation (Fig. S5(a)). In addition, the XRD patterns of N-CQDs before and after the four recycling tests are similar. They could not show any structural modification during the photocatalytic process, demonstrating their good photocorrosive stability(Fig. S5(b)). This result signifies the importance of the designed photocatalyst in the environmental remediation of the targeted pollutants with high reusability potentials.

The kinetic plots obtained by plotting ln (At/Ao) against different time intervals for degradation of various dyes depict a linear correlation which established the fact that removing dyes using N-CQDs as catalysts followed pseudo-first-order kinetics [111] (Fig. S6). The first-order kinetics is expressed in the following equation:
(4)

(
A t
A 0
)
= ln

)

k

Where At and Ao are the initial absorbance and at time t (minute), and kapp is the apparent rate constant. The kapp values of various dyes for the different concentrations are mentioned in Table S2. It was deduced from the rate constant values that the degradation rate of multiple dyes is increased at low concentrations and decreased at higher concentrations [112]. The difference in kinetics rate for different dyes is attributed to their chemical structures, resulting in a difference in adsorption characteristics and susceptibility to photodegradation.

In addition, Table 2 compares the rate constant of the pseudo-first-order kinetic model of synthesized N-CQDs with other reported photocatalysts for the dye degradation, which demonstrated the excellent ability of N-CQDs towards the degradation of TB, XO, AB, and CR dye.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanistic approach towards the degradation dye by N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanism of the deterioration of dyes in the presence of N-CQDs as photocatalysts is illustrated schematically (Fig. S7). Under UV light irradiation, photons of appropriate energy excite electrons from the HOMO level (ground state) of the N-CQDs to the LUMO level (excited state), resulting in the generation of electron-hole pairs. Moreover, the holes and electrons in the valence and conduction band react with the surface adsorbed water, and molecular oxygen dissolves in the solution to produce hydroxy and superoxide radicals [118]. The surface energy traps of high energy density on carbon dots reduced the recombination of charge carriers. As it is highly reactive, the produced reactive oxide species (.O2-) attack the dye molecules and decompose them into nontoxic small molecules [119].

The fluorescence quenching was studied to display the molecular interaction of N-CQDs with the HSL (human serum lysozyme). The binding interaction of N-CQDs with HSL was studied by monitoring the change in the intrinsic emission fluorescence of protein by varying the concentration of N-CQDs. The aromatic amino acid residues in the protein are responsible for intrinsic fluorescence. Fig. 11(a) shows the fluorescence emission spectra of the N-CQDs-HSL complex at 25⁰C at an excitation wavelength of 280 nm. It was observed that with the increasing concentration of N-CQDs, the fluorescence intensity of HSL was decreased, which signifies that the protein is successfully bind to the N-CQDs surface, resulting in a change in the microenvironment of the protein. In addition, conformational changes in HSL were also noticed during the interaction suggesting the availability of more binding sites for N-CQDs. Furthermore, the binding interaction increases the hydrophobic environment of the protein [120]. For bio-macromolecules, fluorescence quenching is broadly categorized as static, dynamic, and static, and dynamic quenching may also co-occur. Static quenching results from the ground state complex formation of fluorophore and quencher, whereas in dynamic quenching, it was due to the collision between the fluorophore and quencher [121,122]. However, the nature of the Stern-Volmer (SV) plot of both quenching will be an upward curvature. The linear SV plot indicates the fluorophores of a single class easily accessible to the quencher. Moreover, at higher concentrations of quencher, the bending of the plot towards the x-axis signifies the proximity of the preferential interaction site between the quencher and fluorophore [57].

The fluorescence quenching mechanism was quantitatively determined by using the Stern-Volmer Eq. (5) [123].
(5)
F o
F
= 1 +
K
s v
[ Q ]
= 1 +
k q
τ o
[ Q ]
Where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of native HSL and N-CQDs-HL complex, Q & τ0 are the concentration of the quencher and average lifetime of N-CQDs without quencher, Ksv & Kq are quenching, and bimolecular quenching constant.

The Ksv value for the N-CQDs-HL complex, as determined from the slope of the SV plot, was found to be 0.58 × 105 M−1, as shown in Fig. 11(b). Due to the molecular interactions, the quenching state between the fluorophore and quencher was purely static and dynamic. Nevertheless, these two states are extremes, and in most cases, the deviation of the SV plot from linearity (upward curvature) pointed toward the existence of both static and dynamic quenching, which co-occurs as stated above. In our study, an upward curvature is observed in the SV plot drawn, indicating the existence of both static and dynamic quenching.

In summary, synthesizing N-CQDs using grass via a simple hydrothermal method obeys the principle of green chemistry as it is cost-effective and without toxic chemicals. This study provided a renewable and sustainable strategy for designing a photocatalyst by converting waste into value-added products. The peak at 22.4° in XRD confirmed the amorphous nature of N-CQDs. The elemental composition of N-doped CDs, as determined by XPS analysis, was found to be C (70%), O (20%), and N (10%), respectively. The absence of any other residual element highlights the purity of N-CQDs. FTIR analysis manifested the existence of functional groups (O-H, C=O, CH2, and N-H) in the product responsible for the outstanding aqueous dispersibility of the N-CQDs. XPS results complement the results of FT-IR. As estimated using Image-J software, the average size of N-CQDs was determined to be 3.4 nm. Furthermore, the product exhibits good quantum yield with promising photoluminescent properties. The photodegradation efficiency of different dyes shows good performance at pH 4 and 9 when the photocatalyst dosage is 0.1 g. The maximum degradation efficiency at varying concentrations (5, 10, 50 and 100 ppm) was found to be 98, 96.66, 94.16 and 93% for TB, 90, 85.56, 74.12 and 73% for XO, 90.13, 88.11, and 75% for AB, 90.76, 90.13, 84.8, and 78.2% for CR dye respectively, which demonstrated the excellent photocatalytic activity of N-CQDs. The high photocatalytic activity of N-CQDs' is attributed to their activeness under visible and UV radiation. The pseudo-first-order rate equation further quantifies the photocatalyst's photodegradation efficiency of dyes. In addition, the structural changes in protein were observed upon interaction with N-CQDs, as observed from the fluorescence spectra. The modified Stern-Volmer constant was determined to be 0.58 × 105 M−1. Hence, N-CQDs as photocatalyst has excellent potential for extending industrial application in removing pollutant, and in vivo nanomedical applications.

: Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Anees Ahmad: Supervision, Conceptualization, Resources, Project administration, Writing – review & editing. Hesam Salimi Shahraki: Formal analysis, Validation. Nimra Shakeel: Formal analysis, Validation. Rani Bushra: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Formal analysis.
